coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with selective fluorescence response to hydroxyl radical for sensitive detection of bleomycin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Xiaoqin
Zhang, Menghan
Wang, Yao
Li, Congpei
Zhang, Xintian
Weng, Shaohuang
Li, Yiming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, antioxidant carbon dots (ACDs) with high quantum yield (63.98%) were developed through a one-step strategy using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and L-arginine as sources. The ACDs demonstrated excellent in vitro antioxidant activity. Additionally, ACDs displayed a unique fluorescence response to hydroxyl radical (OH). Leveraging this distinctive feature, a selective and sensitive fluorescence-based sensor was developed for detecting bleomycin (BLM) in the presence of ferrous ions (Fe2+). The studies on the oxidizing properties and dynamic quenching mechanism of ACDs by OH suggested that OH reduced the contents of the amino and hydroxyl groups of ACDs, leading to a decreased electron cloud density of ACDs for dynamic quenching of ACDs. Under optimally refined conditions, the detection limit of this strategy for BLM was 0.58 μg/mL, with a linear range spanning from 1 to 24 μg/mL when employing ACDs as the probe. This fluorescence sensing strategy provides a promising method for the detection of BLM in clinical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antioxidant property
Fluorescence response
Hydroxyl radical
Bleomycin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Free radicals scavenging activity of ACDs
BLM assay in human plasma samples
The evaluation of the antioxidant activity of ACDs
The detecting feasibility of BLM using ACDs as probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, HRTEM (top right) and particle size distribution of ACDs (top left). (B) UV–Vis spectrum and fluorescence spectra of ACDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of ACDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) FTIR spectrum of ACDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of DPPH·(A) and ABTS·+ (C) in the presence of different concentrations of ACDs, and the inhibition rates of DPPH·(B) and ABTS·+ (D) by different concentrations of ACDs and AA.

(A) UV–Vis spectra of oxTMB from different system. (B) EPR spectra of ·OH after reaction of Fe2+ with H2O2 and BLM at 3 and 15 min, respectively. (C) Feasibility analysis for the detection of BLM. (D) Fluorescence response of ACDs to different radicals (·OH, H2O2, 1O2, NO2 −, O2
−, ONOO−).

(A) Fluorescence decay curves of ACDs, ACDs-BLM, ACDs-Fe2+, and ACDs-BLM-Fe2+. (B) The changes of F0/F of ACDs-BLM-Fe2+ at different concentrations of BLM under different temperatures. High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (C), C1s (D) of ACDs before (upper) and after (down) the reaction with ·OH.

(A) Effect of interferents on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra and (C) QE of the detection system at different concentrations of BLM. The inset of (C) shows the linear relationship between the QE and BLM concentration. (D) The black bar indicates the QE of 32 independent measurements of BLM at 25 μg/mL, and the red bar and red error bar indicate the mean and SD of the 32 measurements, respectively.
The preparation process of ACDs and the strategy of using ACDs as fluorescent probes for BLM detection.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with selective fluorescence response to hydroxyl radical for sensitive detection of bleomycin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Emergency, The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Regional Medical Center, Binhai Campus of the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350212, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Binhai Campus of the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Regional Medical Center, Binhai Campus of the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350212, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Emergency, The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350005, China (Y. Li).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Antioxidant carbon dots (ACDs) with high quantum yield were designed for the effective detection of bleomycin (BLM) with the presence of ferrous ions (Fe2+) ascribed to the specific fluorescence response of ACDs to hydroxyl radical (
OH).

In this work, antioxidant carbon dots (ACDs) with high quantum yield (63.98%) were developed through a one-step strategy using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and L-arginine as sources. The ACDs demonstrated excellent in vitro antioxidant activity. Additionally, ACDs displayed a unique fluorescence response to hydroxyl radical (OH). Leveraging this distinctive feature, a selective and sensitive fluorescence-based sensor was developed for detecting bleomycin (BLM) in the presence of ferrous ions (Fe2+). The studies on the oxidizing properties and dynamic quenching mechanism of ACDs by OH suggested that OH reduced the contents of the amino and hydroxyl groups of ACDs, leading to a decreased electron cloud density of ACDs for dynamic quenching of ACDs. Under optimally refined conditions, the detection limit of this strategy for BLM was 0.58 μg/mL, with a linear range spanning from 1 to 24 μg/mL when employing ACDs as the probe. This fluorescence sensing strategy provides a promising method for the detection of BLM in clinical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a subclass of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, renowned for their array of distinctive qualities, including impeccable biocompatibility, controllable preparation, easy functionalization, and unwavering luminescence stability[1,2]. CDs have found extensive applications spanning various domains, including sensing [3], bioimaging [4,5], and biotherapy [6]. Currently, CDs can be efficiently synthesized through a range of methodologies, such as the hydrothermal strategy, microwave radiation and melting methods using small molecules and biomass as raw materials [7–9]. The choice of carbon precursor is one of the crucial factors that affect the performance of CDs [10]. Specific raw materials can be combined with appropriate synthesis techniques to achieve elemental doping and active functional group modification, thereby enhancing the fluorescence performance or specific functionalization of CDs [11,12]. For example, our group previously reported that specifically synthesized CDs weakly respond to most metal ions but are highly responsive to the complex of Cu2+ and deferasirox for the selective detection of deferasirox [13]. Additionally, we obtained green-emitting CDs with large Stokes shift by doping with N and F elements, which could detect tigecycline with the avoidance of interference from real blood samples [14]. Moreover, antioxidant CDs doped with Zn/Cu demonstrated excellent SOD/CAT enzyme activity and could enhance electron transfer ability for the treatment of myocardial ischemia–reperfusion injury [15]. Although CDs have made significant progress in response to specific targets based on their optical properties, there is a need for further exploration to adjust the functional groups of CDs and their optical properties to achieve extended biomedical applications.

Fluorescent analytical techniques, which are predicated on the utilization of fluorescent dyes or fluorescent nanomaterials, frequently necessitate the modulation of signals to facilitate the detection of specific analytes through the action of reducing agents, such as glutathione and ascorbic acid [16]. The redox reactions involving reducing agents and MnO2 nanosheets has been widely developed for various applications, including target testing and drug delivery [17–19]. Moreover, the change of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is closely related to many biological processes [20]. In general, ROS includes superoxide anion (O2
−), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) [21]. Recently, researchers have made gradual strides in the development of methodologies that engage nanomaterials to respond to, eliminate, or induce changes in ROS levels [22–24]. Among them, the use of CDs as fluorescent probes or antioxidants in response to ROS have received increasing attentions [25–27]. For example, red-emitting CDs synthesized from o-phenylenediamine and catechol were applied to test the radicals of O2
−, ·OH or 1O2 through signal-off strategy [27]. CDs prepared from Scutellaria barbata and Herba Hedyotis diffusae can scavenge a variety of reactive oxygen species and nitrogen radicals [28]. The applications of CDs in ROS-related work are focused on the ROS imaging through fluorescence changes or the removal of ROS by exerting antioxidant capacity [29–31]. However, there are few reports on CDs with antioxidant activity in response to self-fluorescence changes caused by one type of radical. It is evident that the development of facile and timely fluorescent methodologies employing CDs with antioxidant prowess, specifically in response to a specific radical, holds significant promise in expanding the scope of applications for CDs.

Bleomycin (BLM), a glycopeptide natural antibiotic isolated from streptomyces rotundus [32], holds a pivotal role in clinical practice for the chemotherapy of various malignant tumors [33,34]. The antitumor mechanism of BLM is mainly attributed to the ability of BLM to cause the cleavage of specific sequences of DNA or RNA when oxygen and low-valent transition metals (Fe2+ or Cu2+) are used as cofactors [35,36]. However, excessive and inappropriate use of BLM can cause serious side effects of nephrotoxicity and pulmonary toxicity, which may eventually lead to severe pulmonary fibrosis [37]. Therefore, there is a need to develop a reliable, sensitive and immediate method for BLM monitoring during administration to minimize these side effects.

Compared with high performance liquid chromatography [38], radioimmunoassay [39] and electrochemical methods [40] used for BLM analysis, fluorescence analysis has demonstrated great potential to become an important method for rapid and timely drug analysis because of its rapid and sensitive advantages [41]. Using the interaction between N-GQDs and single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) as the responsive element, a fluorescence method based on the BLM-Fe2+ induced ssDNA strand breaks is developed for sensitive detecting BLM [36]. However, most of the current fluorometric methods are based on the selective cleavage of fluorescence labeled DNA probes by BLM-Fe2+ complexes for BLM detection. Scarce attention has been given to the development of assays employing nanoprobes endowed with antioxidant properties and utilizing these probes for the direct detection of BLM.

Herein, using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and L-arginine as functionalized raw materials, antioxidant carbon dots (ACDs) with excellent antioxidant performance were prepared through a one-step method. The ACDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence ability with the surface rich in amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Compared with other ROS radicals, ACDs have selective and sensitive fluorescence response to ·OH. Based on the principle that BLM can generate ·OH in the presence of ferrous ions and oxygen, a fluorescence sensor with ACDs as the probe was constructed. This approach capitalized on the oxidative properties of ·OH and the ACDs' innate ability to interact with them, thereby instigating changes in the surface functional groups of the ACDs, in turn influencing their fluorescence. And the sensitive detection of BLM was achieved by the dynamic quench of ACDs and ·OH with well analytical performance (Scheme 1 ).

2, 2′-Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS), sodium chloride (NaCl), L-tyrosine (L-Tyr), L-cysteine (L-Cys), streptomycin (SM) and gentamycin (GM) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., (Shanghai, China). Bleomycin (BLM), 2, 2-Dip-henyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), erythromycin (EM) and vancomycin (VCA) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., (Shanghai, China). Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), ethylenediamine, L-arginine (L-Arg) and glucose were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., (Shanghai, China). Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Xilong Science Co., (Shantou, China). Vincristine (VCR) was obtained from Renhe Hetero Pharmaceutical Co., (Zhengzhou, China). Cisplatin (DDP) was purchased from Jiangsu Haosen Pharmaceutical Group Co., (Lianyungang, China). Adriamycin (ADM) was purchased from Hanhui Pharmaceutical Co., (Hangzhou, China). Ultrapure water was used in the total work.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected by NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Ultraviole-visible absorption spectra were recorded with UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The morphology of CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using Tecnai F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI Company, USA).

Briefly, 1.8 g of citric acid monohydrate, 0.7 g of L-arginine and 2 mL of ethylenediamine were mixed in 18 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was hydrothermally reacted at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reacting solution was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane and purified through dialysis (500–1000 Da) for 48 h. After freeze-drying, the ACDs was stored in a dryer for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radicals scavenging activity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The scavenging activity of ACDs against DPPH · was determined by adding different concentrations of ACDs (0–200 μg/mL) to DPPH · ethanol solution in the dark for 1 h. The absorbance of DPPH · was then measured at 525 nm.

ABTS ·+ was obtained by mixing ABTS solution (14 mM) with K2S2O8 solution (5.6 mM) in a 1:1 vol ratio of in the dark for 12 h. Different concentrations of ACDs (0–200 μg/mL) were reacted with ABTS ·+ in the dark for 30 min, and the absorbance was then measured at 728 nm.

The QY of ACDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QS, dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54.6%) as a reference. Suitable concentrations of ACDs and quinine sulfate solutions were prepared. The absorbance of ACDs and QS at 345 nm was similar and not greater than 0.05. The fluorescence emission spectra of the above solutions were measured under 345 nm excitation. The QY of ACDs was calculated according to the following equation:
QY
ACDs
=
QY
Qs
K
ACDs
K
Qs
η
ACDs
η
Qs
2

QYCDs and QYQS are the quantum yields of ACDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. η is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 0.1 M H2SO4). K is the slope of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curve [42].

The 20 μL of ACDs (4 μg/mL) and 20 μL of Fe2+ (200 μM) were mixed and reacted with BLM solutions of different concentrations. The reacted solution was diluted to be 200 μL using PBS (pH 4), and named ACDs-BLM-Fe2+. After reacting at 30 ℃ for 50 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of ACDs-BLM-Fe2+ were recorded under excitation at 345 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of (F0-F)/F0 was used as a quantitative standard. F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of the mixture of ACDs and Fe2+ (ACDs-Fe2+) in the absence and presence of BLM at 440 nm, respectively.

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of this method for clinical sample detection, antitumor drugs and common substances that were used in combination with BLM were selected as interfering substances (ADM, DDP, VCR, SM, GM, EM, VCA, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, L-Arg, L-Tyr, L-Cys, and Glu). These interfering substances were added into ACDs-Fe2+ to evaluate the fluorescence changes in accord with the procedure in section 2.5

The NO2 − was obtained by dissolving NaNO2 in ultrapure water. H2O2 stock solution was obtained from commercial aqueous solution. The superoxide solution (O2 ·−) was prepared by adding 1 mg of KO2 to 1 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide with vigorous stirring for 10 min. Singlet oxygen (1O2) was obtained by mixing 2 mM H2O2 with 20 mM NaClO. The ·OH was obtained from the addition of 2 mM FeSO4·7H2O into 6 mM H2O2. The concentration of ·OH was equaled to that of ferrous ion. The ONOO− solution was prepared according to the reported method [43]. A mixture of NaNO2 (0.6 M) and H2O2 (0.7 M) was acidified with HCl (0.6 M), and then NaOH (1.5 M) was added to make the solution alkaline within 1–2 s. The concentration of ONOO− was determined by measuring the absorbance at 302 nm [ CONOO −=Abs302nm/1.67(mM)]. The 20 μL of ACDs (4 μg/mL) were mixed with 20 μL of free radical stock solution (200 μM), respectively. The reacted solution was diluted to be 200 μL using PBS (pH 4). After reacting at 37 ℃ for 20 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of ACDs were recorded under excitation at 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BLM assay in human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: Plasma samples from healthy individuals were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University. Acetonitrile was added to the plasma samples at a volume ratio of 1:1 in 2 mL centrifuge tube. Then, the supernatant was removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. A 5-fold diluted plasma (20 μL) was added to ACDs-Fe2+, which contained 20 μL ACDs (4 μg/mL) and 20 μL Fe2+ (200 μM). After adding different concentrations of BLM and diluting to 200 μL with PBS, the spiked recoveries were tested.

The ACDs were found to have a quasi-spherical particle shape, with an average size of 2.77 nm as observed by TEM (Fig. 1 A). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.35 nm, indicating the presence of graphite structures [44]. The optical properties of ACDs were characterized. The absorption spectra showed two peaks at 238 nm and 344 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and the n-π* transition of the carbonyl/carboxyl group [45], respectively (Fig. 1B). The excitation-independent fluorescence spectra exhibited an optimal excitation wavelength of 345 nm and an emission center at 440 nm (Fig. 1C). The fluorescence QY of ACDs was calculated to be 63.98% using quinine sulfate as reference standard (Fig. S1).

The surface functional groups of ACDs were studied by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum displayed peaks at 3442 cm−1, 1660 cm−1, 1553 cm−1, and 1390 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H, C = O, the bending vibration of N–H, and the stretching vibration of C-N, respectively (Fig. 1D). The XPS analysis revealed three peaks at 284.19 eV, 399.79 eV and 530.44 eV, attributing to the characteristic binding energy signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S2A). The elemental contents were 62.07% (C), 17.17% (N) and 20.76% (O), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S2B) suggested the three fitted peaks belonging to C–C (284.72 eV), C-N/C-O (286.03 eV), and C = O (287.75 eV) [46], respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s illustrated three dominant peaks at 399.28 eV, 399.70 eV, and 401.06 eV, corresponding to pyrrole nitrogen, graphite nitrogen, and N–H [47], respectively (Fig. S2C). The deconvoluted XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. S2D) showed the two peaks at 530.76 eV, and 531.55 eV, corresponding to C = O, and C-OH [48], respectively. The combined results of FTIR and XPS confirmed the presence of functional groups of –NH2, –OH and –COOH on the surface of ACDs.

The stability of the fluorescent property of ACDs in various environments was investigated. After continuous excitation at 345 nm for 1 h, the fluorescence intensities of ACDs remained basically unchanged, indicating the anti-photobleaching ability of ACDs (Fig. S3A). ACDs were also found to exhibit good stability in different temperatures and variable concentrations of salt (Fig. S3B and S3C). The fluorescence intensity remaining stable at 4–85 ℃ and higher than 96.2% under different salt concentrations (Fig. S3B and S3C). Furthermore, except for the unstable fluorescence intensity in strong acidic conditions, ACDs showed almost stable fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 4–11 (Fig. S3D). The long-term stability of ACDs was demonstrated by the fact that the fluorescence intensity of ACDs stored for 90 days remained above 94.1% (Fig. S3E). These results show that ACDs exhibit good optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The evaluation of the antioxidant activity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The DPPH · and ABTS ·+ are two stable free radicals that are used as indicators to evaluate the antioxidant activity of nanomaterials [49]. The absorption peak of DPPH · was typical observed at 525 nm, and its peak decreased as the contents of ACDs increased (Fig. 2 A), suggesting a reduction in the DPPH· content. Using L-ascorbic acid (AA) as the control, the half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of ACDs and AA against DPPH · were 40.72 μg/mL and 12.33 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 2B). The peak intensities of ABTS ·+ at 728 nm also decreased gradually as the increased amounts of ACDs (Fig. 2C), indicating the scavenging ability of ACDs to ABTS ·+ . Moreover, compared with the conventional antioxidant reagent of AA, the scavenging rate of ACDs had a similar scavenging trend with AA. The IC50 of ACDs and AA were 12.62 μg/mL and 3.72 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 2D). These results indicate that ACDs have significant scavenging activity against DPPH · and ABTS ·+ and can serve as an effective antioxidant medium, such as the clearance performance of ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detecting feasibility of BLM using ACDs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: The possible generation of ·OH of the mixture of BLM and Fe2+ (BLM- Fe2+) was verified using TMB as a specific probe. Results showed that there were no absorption peaks observed in TMB, TMB and Fe2+, and TMB and BLM, but there was a clear absorption peak at 652 nm in the TMB and BLM- Fe2+ system (Fig. 3 A). Using the classical Fenton reaction of H2O2 and Fe2+ as comparison, the occurrence of the absorption peek of oxTMB at 652 nm was consistent with the result of the introduction of TMB in BLM-Fe2+, suggesting the formed ·OH in BLM-Fe2+. Using 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent for ·OH, the EPR signal of DMPO/·OH after the reaction of BLM with Fe2+ was enhanced with the increase of reaction time, which was similar to the change of EPR signal for ·OH production by the Fenton reaction, indicating the produced ·OH by the reaction of BLM and Fe2+ (Fig. 3B).

The fluorescence response of ACDs to ·OH were examined in BLM-Fe2+. The fluorescence of ACDs was not quenched by BLM or Fe2+ alone. While, the fluorescence was significantly weakened after the interaction with BLM-Fe2+ (Fig. 3C), indicating that the ·OH generated by BLM-Fe2+ can quench the fluorescence of ACDs. It is feasible to monitor the content of BLM by the change of the fluorescence intensities of ACDs with the presence of Fe2+. Furthermore, the specificity of the fluorescence response of ACDs to other radicals was examined. Compared with the strong response to ·OH, ACDs exhibited little response to other kinds of ROS radicals (Fig. 3D), suggesting the specific fluorescence response of ACDs to ·OH for testing.

The detection mechanism of BLM using ACDs as probes was investigated. The fluorescence lifetime curves of ACDs, ACDs and BLM (ACDs-BLM), ACDs and Fe2+ (ACDs-Fe2+), ACDs and the mixture of BLM and Fe2+(ACDs-BLM-Fe2+) were examined and compared. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of ACDs, ACDs-BLM, and ACDs-Fe2+ were almost the same of 13.82 ns, 13.57 ns, and 13.46 ns. While, the fluorescence lifetime of ACDs decreased from 13.82 ns to 10.01 ns after reacting with ·OH (Fig. 4 A). The decreased fluorescence lifetime suggested the dynamic quenching of ACDs in BLM-Fe2+ [50]. Then, the Stern-Volmer equation of F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q] was operated [51] (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of probe without and with quenching agent. [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Ksv is the dynamic quenching constant). As shown in Fig. 4B, the Ksv increased with the temperature increasing from 30 to 50 °C. The variable and increased Ksv confirmed the dynamic quenching mechanism of ACDs by ·OH in ACDs-BLM-Fe2+.

Typically, a long conjugated molecular structure that is rich in substituents, such as –NH2, –OH, –OCH3, –CN, etc., that increase the degree of π-electron conjugation, can increase the fluorescence efficiency of the substance. The N atom in NH2 can increase the electron cloud density of the entire π conjugated domain, while reducing the non-radiative recombination center of CDs, thereby increasing the QY of CDs [52]. The functional groups of ACDs before and after the interaction with ·OH were compared using XPS (Fig. 4C and 4D). From the integrated areas of N–H (35.99%) and C-O/C-N (33.36%) (Table S1 and S2) and the relative QYACDs of 63.98%, the strong fluorescence efficiency of ACDs may be positively correlated with the content of amino and hydroxyl functional groups present on the surfaces. After the reaction of ACDs with ·OH, the integrated areas of N1s and C1s from XPS showed that the amino and hydroxyl groups decreased from 35.99% to 15.76% and 33.36% to 18.98%, respectively (Table S1 and S2). The dynamic quenching of ACDs with ·OH may be caused by the covalent interaction of ·OH with the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of ACDs, which destroyed and reduced the electron cloud density in the conjugation plane and π domains of ACDs, thereby causing the fluorescence quenching of ACDs.

The detecting conditions were optimized. As shown in Fig. S4A, the quenching efficiency (QE) initially increased and then decreased with the gradually increased ACDs. And the QE reached the maximum degree when the concentration of ACDs was 4 μg/mL. The concentration of Fe2+ played a crucial role in the formation of ·OH. As the increased concentrations of Fe2+, the QE gradually increased and then stabilized after reaching 200 μM (Fig. S4B). Considering that ACDs are unstable in luminescence at pH less than 3 and Fe2+ is unstable in alkaline environment, the optimal pH was examined within a range of 4–9. In Fig. S4C, the QE of ACDs decreased and then increased with an increase in pH, and the QE was optimal at pH 4. The QE was almost the same in the temperature range of 25-55℃, so 30℃ was chosen as the optimal reaction temperature (Fig. S4D). Moreover, when the reaction time was within the range of 15–80 min, the QE gradually increased and then became stable (Fig. S4E). Based on the convenience of the detection, the time was set at 50 min. Overall, the testing system operated at pH 4, 30℃ for 50 min with the concentrations of ACDs and Fe2+ being 4 μg/mL and 200 μM, respectively.

The specificity of the assay for BLM was initially evaluated by examining the effects of potential interfering substances in blood, as well as co-administered antitumor drugs such as adriamycin, cisplatin, and vincristine. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the effects of the interfering substances on the fluorescence of ACDs with the presence of Fe2+ were weak, indicating the high specificity of this method for BLM detection.

Under the optimal reaction conditions, the fluorescence intensity of ACDs at 440 nm gradually decreased as the concentrations of BLM increased (Fig. 5B). The QE ((F0-F)/F0) of ACDs was gradually enhanced. The QE showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of BLM in the range of 1–24 μg/mL, with a linear equation of Y = 0.01646 CBLM + 0.01368 (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 5C). The detection limit was calculated to be 0.58 μg/mL using 3σ/S (where σ is the SD of the blank and S is the slope of the linear fit equation, n = 10).

The reproducibility of the method was evaluated by measuring 32 BLM samples, with four samples measured per day for eight consecutive days. As shown in Fig. 5D, the QE of 32 samples was close, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the measurements was 3.04%. The RSD of the inter-day measurements was 2.37%, and the RSD of the intra-day measurements was in the range of 1.01%-3.42%, indicating good reproducibility of this method.

To further assess the accuracy of this method for the determination of BLM in blood samples, the spiked recoveries of BLM in plasma were determined. As shown in Table S3, the spiked recoveries of BLM were in the range of 90.98%-108.04% with the RSD less than 2.0%, indicating the applicability of the present fluorescence sensing method for the practical detection of BLM in actual samples.

In summary, based on the advantages of easy functionalization of ACDs, we designed and synthesized ACDs with antioxidant activity and specific response to hydroxyl radicals. ACDs showed good scavenging ability against DPPH·and ABTS ·+ and had anti-oxidative stress potential. Inspired by the fact that BLM interacts with metal ions to generate hydroxyl radicals, the fluorescence response of ACDs with the interaction with BLM-Fe2+ was applied for BLM selective and sensitive detection. The multifunctionalization of ACDs on the basis of antioxidant property and fluorescent response will provide a new insight for the treatment and detection of oxidative stress-related diseases or drugs.

Xiaoqin Deng: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Menghan Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yao Wang: Data curation. Congpei Li: Resources. Xintian Zhang: Formal analysis. Shaohuang Weng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yiming Li: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2021Y9007), and the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022J01493).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123582.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
